chunks.title: Carnivore Diet Benefits
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the benefits of the carnivore diet, particularly about increasing testosterone levels, energy levels, and reversing aging effects. Personal testimonials highlight dramatic health improvements, including a notable decrease in depression and hair restoration.
chunks.transcript: testosterone and energy says cheyet p uh as a review of heart and soil supplements this one kicks ass guys listen to this he says i eat carnivore meat water salt fruit honey and have been doing this for two years i feel amazing but adding in these organ supplements from hardened soil i feel like my testosterone is through the roof i have so much energy and clarity of mind i've been playing around every month with different combinations my favorite so far is liver and bone marrow bone matrix and mood memory and brain those three together thanks to my wife for finding the carnivore diet two years ago i have reversed aging wtf i have less gray hairs on my head face and chest i had a receding hairline that is now grown in and most importantly that my depression went away 100 and so many other amazing things i've noticed about myself it is unbelievable what we can change for the better just changing what you eat thanks to paul and everyone at heart and soil for a clean effective product and transparency you guys rock i would agree with that the folks at heart and soil rock i'm so proud to have built this company you can find us at heartandsoil.co.com more organs in your life either fresh or desiccated this combination was bone marrow and liver moon memory and brain and bone matrix for cheyet that really helped him rock it uh and crush it again this is how you reclaim your birthright to radical optimal health getting organs along with your animal-based diet of

chunks.title: Introduction to Gut Health
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces the topic of gastrointestinal (GI) health, discussing the significance of having a healthy gut microbiome and potential conditions that affect it. He mentions a range of digestive disorders like SIBO, IBS, and IBD.
chunks.transcript: cast did a shirtless solo cast and i talked about gut stuff and i went down the rabbit hole i just did a full summary of gi stuff how it goes wrong what it looks like when it goes wrong what a healthy gut is things that cause it to go wrong specific medical conditions sibo ibs inflammatory bowel disease gerd gastroesophageal reflux disease gallstones and how to fix them and then how to optimize your gut from my perspective so there's a lot in here let this be the most recent up-to-date summarized how to get your gut to kick ass episode hope you guys enjoy this one on to the podcast this week on the podcast i want to do a gut episode i want to talk about all things gi gastrointestinal related a gut redux i want to talk about what a healthy gut and a healthy gut microbiome means how we define it what the limitations of our understanding of that are what can damage our guts how we go wrong conditions that involve damaged guts things like small intestinal bacterial overgrowth sibo inflammatory bowel diseases crohn's and ulcerative colitis ibs even things like gastroesophageal reflux i want to talk about all of those and

chunks.title: Understanding a Healthy Gut
chunks.summary: The speaker outlines what constitutes a healthy gut, emphasizing the absence of symptoms like mood disorders, digestive issues, and inflammatory conditions. He critiques the traditional metrics often used in Western medicine to define gut health.

chunks.title: Critique of Fiber in Diet
chunks.summary: The speaker challenges the mainstream view that vegetable fiber is essential for gut health. He argues that fiber from plant foods can be harmful and instead advocates for fiber sourced from fruits and animal products.
chunks.transcript: individual so i think that all definitions of a healthy gut must start with the health of the individual if you are a healthy individual that means you are free from mood disorders yes mood swings depression anxiety these can be related to gut you are free from brain fog you are free from autoimmune conditions whether it be skin conditions like eczema psoriasis you are free from other autoimmune conditions hashimoto's thyroiditis you're free from rheumatoid arthritis you're free from all of the autoimmune conditions including the autoimmune conditions of the gut inflammatory bowel diseases crohn's ulcerative colitis i would also argue that irritable bowel syndrome is a type of autoimmune disease of the gut as is small intestinal bacterial overgrowth or sibo which i will talk about in more detail later so i think that you also must be free from gastrointestinal reflux disease gerd reflux which is probably the most rampant gi issue facing our populations proton pump inhibitors are a symptomatic treatment of that that lowers the acid production of the stomach by raising the ph of the stomach it doesn't actually stop reflux it just stops you from reflexing acid you reflux alkaline or more alkaline contents into your esophagus which means that you have trouble digesting your food because humans homo sapiens have evolved with a very acidic gut we know this about our history where we've come from anthropologically evolutionarily is the fact that we have very acidic guts suggesting we were eating carry on we were eating rotting things including meat mostly meat we were scavengers we are protected in some ways by our very acidic stomachs from contaminants in our external environment which we take into our bodies when we eat and if you raise the ph of your gut by making it less acid then you are making yourself more susceptible and indeed there are studies which show strong correlations between use of proton pump inhibitors and things like community acquired pneumonia even dementia probably related to nutrient deficiencies that compound long term because guess what you need an acidic stomach the way your body was designed to absorb the nutrients in your food so if you take proton from inhibitors or if you take h2 blockers which is another type of medication that can lower the acid content of your stomach by raising the ph you are also going to inhibit the absorption the assimilation of nutrients in your body that is a big thing correct the root cause my friends that is what i always say so you must be free from all of those conditions to have a healthy gut if you are a healthy individual and i deliberately started with mood if you have a good mood if you have good sleep if you have good libido if you have good insulin sensitivity because we know that there are changes in the microbiome including dysbiosis which is a fancy word for overgrowth of the wrong type of bacteria basically synonymous with weeds in the garden in human guts that are associated with things like diabetes and other insulin resistant states they're basically all the same insulin resistance is insulin resistance aka metabolic dysfunction so to have a healthy gut you must be a healthy individual free from all of those conditions i listed earlier plus gas bloating constipation you should have darn good poops every day that are easy to pass without straining without blood without bleeding on the toilet paper whether it's from hemorrhoids or other sources you should have a very pleasant experience when you go to take a poop your poop should not be excessively stinky you should not have tons of farts all the time you shouldn't get gas and bloating after meals that make you want to scurry away and go fart in private if you do all of those things then you probably have a healthy gut and you don't need to go digging massively into issues that could be happening in your gut if you don't have a problem with your gut don't fix something that's already good but that i think is how we define a healthy gut symptomatically more subjectively with some symptoms to correlate with that now that is not always how western medicine looks at it we like to add technical metrics which i think can be misleading but it gives us something objective to look at the most commonly used metric for a quote unquote healthy gut which has its limitations is alpha diversity which is a fancy to a zoological term for the number of different species within a given ecosystem or niche within an ecosystem so alpha diversity gets brought up a lot when it comes to fiber but i think that the main indicators of a healthy gut are how you feel and how you look and we can basically end the discussion right there if we're making any sort of comparison between a vegan diet and an animal-based diet because i've never met a vegan who didn't fart massively i did certainly when i was a vegan um and that would disqualify them based on at least carnivore md's criteria for a healthy gut because you shouldn't be farting all the time when i was a vegan i was a raw vegan for seven months guys i don't do anything lightly i couldn't even go to a movie theater in fact i kind of liked movie theaters because they had the squishy seats and i could fart into the squishy seats for a few hours and then when i got up nobody really knew who farted because i just it all got released from the seat but if i was in a room with couches that weren't squishy and there wasn't anywhere anywhere to fart i was in trouble because i had a lot of gas when i was a vegan i think most vegans do and they've become so deluded in some ways that they celebrated and say you should be farting a lot so that is the beginning of that conversation but all of this is wrapped up in the notion of how do we get a healthy gut how do we care for our guts how do we create the healthiest gut possible and within most communities within most nutritional circles now the answer to that question is more fiber predominantly more fiber from vegetables this is where i begin to have significant disagreements with the mainstream pundits in the nutrition space because i don't think the vegetable fiber is a necessary thing for a healthy gut at all and think in fact conversely the most vegetable fiber is going to damage your gut and cause issues people who argue for beneficial for the human gut in any way shape or form so why have we been told for so long that vegetable fiber is the answer like so many places where western medicine and mainstream nutrition has they find is that kids in rural areas eat more fiber but they have a greater alpha diversity in their gut well what we find when we do interventional

chunks.title: Impact of Dietary Choices on Health
chunks.summary: The discussion turns to the impact of dietary choices on different health conditions, including autoimmune diseases and GI disorders like SIBO and IBD. The speaker suggests that animal-based diets can help alleviate symptoms and improve overall gut health.
chunks.transcript: studies which must always be done to test the hypotheses generated from observational studies we can't end in interventional studies like um so many nutritional authorities would want us to we must test them interventional studies when we do interventional studies with fiber what we find is giving humans more fiber doesn't change the alpha diversity of the human gut meaning doesn't create more diversity in the gut but i can't tell you how often i hear parroted the notion that eating the rainbow or eating more diverse plant foods equals a more diverse microbiome and that is good but i've never seen a single interventional study to support that notion i've only seen epidemiology which is always disproven when we look at interventional studies so let's look at a couple of those i've spoken about this study a number of times recently it's from justin sonnensburg lab it's from justin sonnenberg's lab at stanford and the title of the study is gut microbiota targeted diets modulate human immune status okay it should have been called plant fiber does nothing for alpha diversity but fermented foods do but that isn't what they called it so if you look here what they did was they had a 17-week randomized protect perspective study designed and they also looked at omics which is a complex method of looking at the types of bacteria in the gut and they looked at the microbiome and the host they did extensive immune profiling and they found distinct effects of each diet what diets were they doing they had a plant-based fiber a vegan type of diet or fermented foods unfortunately i couldn't convince justin to do an animal-based diet that's a joke i wasn't in contact with justin before the study but i wish he had reached out to me because i would have loved to have seen an animal-based diet arm maybe even with some kefir but i suspect i wouldn't be surprised if their hypothesis was that a plant-based fiber diet was going to be beneficial for the gut microbiota with whatever metrics they were using alpha diversity etc what they found was that when they did the studies the plant fiber-based diet did nothing for the alpha diversity but alternatively the high fermented food diet steadily increased microbiota diversity and decreased inflammatory markers this did not happen at all high fiber consumers showed increased gut microbiota encoded glycan degrading caz enzymes despite stable community diversity that basically means the alpha diversity didn't change at all when they gave more plant fiber as i've said many times in the past but they had more enzymes that degrade plant fiber well no [ __ ] if you eat more plant fiber your gut is going to adjust and you're going to have more enzymes in there that degrade plant fiber but from an immunologic perspective the fermented food diet improved inflammatory markers and immune markers while the plant-based fiber diet could do nothing i know that my friend andrew huberman has talked to justin sondenberg about this on his podcast he was actually one of the people that first sent me this study but i don't understand why this hasn't been more talked about in the general nutritional world because it basically disproves the notion that you should be fueled by fiber that plant fiber is going to do anything beneficial for your gut from an immunologic inflammatory or alpha diversity perspective what does improve gut diversity fermented foods where do we find fermented foods on an animal-based diet you are going to get fermented foods from kefir who needs beer when you can have kefir right it's something that i eat every day you can see me eating my breakfast every single day on instagram carnivore md 2.0 you're also going to get quote-unquote natural probiotics from raw dairy which is one of the reasons i enjoy raw dairy i use raw milk from cows or goats to make kefir and i do raw butter and raw cheeses all of those are going to essentially be sources of environmental quote unquote natural probiotics it's not surprising they improve the alpha diversity inflammatory markers and immune markers in the gut in that controlled study from justin sonnenberg's lab other sources of fermented foods as they mentioned in the study are things like kombucha i would be careful of kombuchas that contain large amounts of unfermented sugar in fact kombucha is usually made by adding processed sugar and as i will show you later processed sugar at least in animal models can be very detrimental to the gut so i don't think you should be using processed sugar in contrast honey does have many things which can be beneficial for the gut i'll show you evidence for that later in the podcast you might even consider honey a fermented food because i believe maybe some beekeepers out there will correct me on this but i believe that honey is made in the stomachs in the gi tracts of bees through a process of fermentation so you might even consider honey to be in ways of fermented food kombucha kefir raw cheeses perhaps even yogurts my problem with a lot of yogurt is they contain processed sugars they're often pasteurized i'm not as big a fan of yogurt it's very hard to find high quality yogurt if you make your own great but i think that fermented dairy is a good place to begin with those things what about fermented sauerkraut okay it's probably better than nothing but remember that you are fermenting a cabbage which is a brassica type of plant which has defense chemicals isothiocyanates those are mostly degraded when you ferment it but you are taking a plant that is i believe toxic to humans has plant defense chemicals they are degraded somewhat by the germination process but i still don't think it's the best use of that plant or the best source of fermented foods i would rather have an animal food that is fermented than a plant food that is fermented in my diet i'm not a huge fan of kimchi because it has the capsicum spices as i've talked about in the past those do appear to open tight junctions in cell culture i will show you evidence for that in this podcast these are the hot peppers even sweet peppers do the same thing spicy foods not good for your gut kimchi i don't think it's good for your gut that's why it hurts when you poop it out guys and sauerkraut i think there are much better sources of fermented foods and most of the sauerkraut i've seen it's just a joke in grocery stores it's basically just cabbage soggy cabbage with vinegar why would you eat that maybe if you've got a really really good pickle but again the quality of these in terms of mass production is very poor if you make your own perhaps but i think lacto-fermented dairy is the best choice and i suspect most people many people who have a sensitivity to dairy we'll do better with raw dairy in fact i was talking with a colleague stefan van vliet recently we are going to be collaborating this is super exciting news guys we're going to be do it at utah state university they have a center for nutrition clinical studies and we're going to give patients an animal-based diet but we were talking about the benefits of raw dairy and he was saying that he wants to do a study looking at raw dairy versus conventional dairy i would love to see that study too he also was interested in the differences between those two types of foods i think there's a big difference there though we probably will have trouble using large amounts of raw dairy in our study on an animal-based diet we probably can get raw cheese into it there are other studies that have essentially shown the same thing as justin sonnenberg's study there are other studies that have essentially shown the same thing as justin sonderberg study this one is from harvard university interestingly and they gave patients a fully meat based diet compared to a plant-based diet title of this study diet rapidly and reproducibly alters the human got microbiome this is from nature 2014 what they found in the study was that the alpha diversity did not change at all when patients had zero plant fiber in fact the alpha diversity which you can see at the end of this paper was about the same in both of the studies though the beta diversity improved or increased on a carnivore diet they say no significant differences in alpha diversity were detected on either diet which means that when they gave more plant fiber to these study participants there was no increase in alpha diversity and when they decreased plant fiber significantly there was no uh there was no decrease so you can see here here is fiber intake on the plant-based diet it goes up significantly here it goes down significantly on the carnivore diet the fat intake goes down on the plant-based diet but goes up on the animal-based diet which is actually a carnivore diet so it's different than how i would define animal-based diet protein goes down on the plant-based diet protein goes up on the carnivore diet alpha diversity essentially no significant change on either of these diets a plant-based diet did not increase alpha diversity as somebody would claim and a carnivore diet did not decrease alpha diversity carnivore diet did increase beta diversity which is something that not a lot of people have commented on but beta diversity is a ratio between regional and local species diversity it's probably not worth getting that much in the weeds at this point with regard to this study it's just another one to illustrate the fact that plant-based fiber doesn't give you a very diverse gut and ruling fiber doesn't decrease the diversity of your gut what does increase the diversity of your gut fermented foods and i would also suggest that you can increase the diversity of your gut by being in nature this is something that would explain the observations that i expressed earlier why do the kids in burkina faso who are in rural areas have more alpha diversity than the kids in italy well there's a lot of things that could explain this potentially the kids in burkina faso are eating less processed foods could sugars and seed oils cause issues with the gut yes as i'll show you later in this podcast both processed sugar and seed oils can contribute to dysbiosis that's sort of nebulous term that means overgrowth of the wrong bacteria in the gut weeds in the garden essentially quote unquote but also i believe there is a compelling hypothesis around the possibility that being in nature could contribute to more alpha diversity things like inhaling compounds from trees being around dirt that is in the air these things are real and they affect our microbiomes we know this that being in nature can affect your microbiome so is it possible that we are now dovetailing into something like a hygiene hypothesis that we are overly cleansed today and that we need to get back into nature more and that might affect our alpha diversity yes and as i will show you in a moment

chunks.title: Role of Fermented Foods
chunks.summary: The speaker highlights the benefits of fermented foods in improving gut health, citing studies that demonstrate their effectiveness in enhancing gut microbiota diversity. He discusses various sources of fermented foods and their potential harm from added sugars.

libido do you sleep well do you have good energy for exercise are you happy to be living are you doing a good job with your life then in that case if you're not having gas bloating constipation or gi issues bleeding pain with passing stool and you're getting a good brown snake in the toilet every morning you probably have a healthy gut even if you're eating an animal-based diet that might have more protein than the nutritional pundit du jour wants you to eat more saturated fat which we all know is killing us except when it's not when it's making us healthy humans it appears to be the best thing for us containing many essential nutrients which i'll talk about in the future organs fruit why would you eat vegetable fiber in your diet if you're healthy already a healthy gut is illustrated by how healthfully how vitally you are living your life and we should not become too myopic in our characterization of species in the gut nor should we believe that we need plant fibers from vegetables to get schwarten fatty acids as i mentioned earlier i strongly believe that an animal-based diet surprise surprise organs either fresh or desiccated like hardened soil supplements meat grass-fed primarily fruit as much as you want honey which i'll talk about in a bit has actually good effects on the gut raw dairy for the reasons i mentioned earlier these are the most sought after foods for humans this is why i think an animal-based diet is the optimal diet for humans and that is the majority of humans i don't believe there's a lot of variation in humans i will add as an aside recently that um a carnivore podcaster recently had robert lustig on their podcast and um robert lustig is well known to be a i would say a critic of fructose and sugar and he was asked what about fruit and interestingly robert lustig replied i have no problem with humans eating as much fruit as they want processed sugar is the problem robert lustig's explanation was that the fiber in the fruit prevents the absorption of the fructose i think there are other things at play here because we know that honey something with zero fiber doesn't appear to be damaging for humans either but i would agree with robert lustig on the notion that humans can eat as much fruit as they want just don't eat so much fruit that you become a fruitarian make sure you get your meat and organs first but i found it very interesting that one of the main voices who has spoken out against fructose he's published papers with titles like fructose is alcohol without the buzz he's totally in favor of humans eating as much fruit as possible i'm glad that he was on that podcast and could give some insight into the benefits of fruit to what may be a more dogmatic carnivore community i think so many in the community get get locked into a paradigm where they will not incorporate carbohydrates into their diet and they suffer because of it i know i was there so i want to dive into a little bit of science here in fact a lot of science here i've got a good number of articles i want to share with you guys but i don't want this podcast to go too long so i want to talk about what damages the gut share some literature that i think supports my concerns for that and then talk about certain conditions and then i will talk about how i think we reverse that and how we can heal our guts and then give ourselves the foundation to become freaking awesome radical humans there are many things that damage the gut i think that plant foods predominantly vegetables can contain many defense chemicals like lectins carbohydrate binding proteins which can be very damaging to the gut one of the canonical lectins is gluten which i'll talk about in a moment i think this is problematic for both celiacs and non-celiacs though i've been criticized online for showing papers which show inflammation in the guts of celiac disease patients when they are exposed to lectins in a moment i will share papers that suggest there are inflammatory issues for those with non-celiac gluten sensitivity when exposed to gluten as well um and i think it's important to understand that gluten is just one lectin uh of a family of lectins that is quite broad lectins are predominantly found in seeds seeds being seeds nuts grains and beans and these lectins appear to damage the gut something that has not been talked about a whole lot but i think is often overlooked in terms of things that could be potentially harming our guts i also think that other plant compounds as i hinted at earlier capsicum spices even sweet bell peppers hot peppers can damage the gut they can open tight junctions formed by occluding and clot in proteins and release zonulin i will actually show a paper with regards to that in a moment into uh and cause leaky gut which is the colloquial term for a damaged open epithelium a fenestrated gastrointestinal epithelium if you uh will i think that toxins in the environment can probably damage the gut things like glyphosate and pesticides which appear to be in nearly goddamn everything these days um i think that lack of sunlight lack of nutrients can damage the gut there's good evidence that zinc is essential for proper gut permeability or i should say gut integrity and where do we go find zinc that is bioavailable we find it from animal foods good luck getting your zinc from plant foods vegans would try to eat a ton of pumpkin seeds but most of that zinc is going to be bound by phytates by oxalates and as i've shown in the past it is very very poorly bioavailable antibiotics can damage the gut leading to dysbiosis we know that different types of bacteria can also signal to the gut to open gap junctions and cause leaky gut fenestrated gut epithelium i have concerns that artificial sweeteners including stevia and stevia derivatives can damage the gut and it's clear that things like seed oils and processed sugars can also damage the gut so the cards are stacked against us my friends let's start with this paper non-celiac gluten sensitivity triggers gut dysbiosis neuroinflammation gut brain axis dysfunction and vulnerability for dementia that's a bad that's a bad crew that is a motley crew um i do not think humans should mess with gluten even if you are not formally celiac they go on to say in the abstract dysbiosis and non-celiac gluten

chunks.title: Summary and Recommendations
chunks.summary: In conclusion, the speaker reiterates the benefits of an animal-based diet while encouraging listeners to be mindful of what they consume. He promotes sun exposure, limiting processed foods, and incorporating honey and fermented products into one's diet.
